text: All people are created equal declared Thomas Jefferson but all migrants are not. The volume brings together 12 scholars of international renown who provide a historical analysis of how differentiation between migrants occurred in countries such as France, the United States, Turkey, Canada, Mexico, the Netherlands, Sweden and Denmark and how these distinctions were legitimized in policies and public discourses. Together, the chapters in this volume organize themselves into a triptych, each panel dealing with different components of a complex set of questions related to labeling refugees, families and difference. The chapters provided here document in historical perspective the complex interweaving of these issues and how their meanings have changed across time according to different socio-economic and geopolitical contexts as well as the particular characteristics of migrants such as women, working class and disadvantaged ethnic, religious and sexual orientation groups.
